museum-experience_0.title: Abbott Papyrus

artefact.title: Abbott Papyrus
artefact.summary: The Abbott Papyrus serves as an important political document concerning the tomb robberies of the Twentieth Dynasty of Egypt during the New Kingdom . It also gives insight into the scandal between the two rivals Pawero and Paser of Thebes.
artefact.categories: Ancient Egyptian objects in the British Museum; Papyri from ancient Egypt; 2nd-millennium BC manuscripts; 11th-century BC literature

museum-experience_1.title: El-Amra clay model of cattle

artefact.title: El-Amra clay model of cattle
artefact.summary: The El-Amra clay model of cattle is a small ceramic sculpture dating from the Predynastic , Naqada I period in Ancient Egypt , at around 3500 BC . It is one of several models found in graves at El-Amra in Egypt , and is now in the British Museum in London . The model is (at maximum) 8.2 centimetres high, 24.2 cm long and 15.3 cm wide. The model was made from clay , and fired at a low temperature before it was painted, however most of the paint is lost.
artefact.dimensions: Height: 8.2 cm (3.2 in) Length: 24.2 cm (9.5 in) Width: 15.3 cm (6.0 in)
artefact.categories: 4th-millennium BC works; 1901 archaeological discoveries; Ancient Egyptian objects in the British Museum; Sculptures of ancient Egypt; Predynastic Egypt; Ancient Near and Middle East clay objects; Ceramic sculptures; Sculptures of cattle; Amratian culture; Archaeological discoveries in Egypt

museum-experience_2.title: Benin ivory mask

artefact.title: Benin ivory mask
artefact.summary: The Benin ivory mask is a miniature sculptural portrait in ivory of Idia , the first Iyoba ( Queen Mother ) of the 16th century Benin Empire , taking the form of a traditional African mask . [ 1 ] The masks were looted by the British from the palace of the Oba of Benin in the Benin Expedition of 1897 . [ 2 ]
artefact.current_location: Metropolitan Museum of Art , British Museum , Seattle Art Museum , Linden Museum , private collection
artefact.categories: African sculptures in the British Museum; Sculptures in the Metropolitan Museum of Art; Ethnographic objects in the British Museum; Ivory works of art; Masks in Africa; African queen mothers; Ritual masks; Sculptures of women in New York City; Collection of the Seattle Art Museum; Cultural depictions of queens; Benin Court Art; Sculptures of women in London; Sculptures of women in Nigeria

museum-experience_3.title: Bronze Head of Queen Idia

artefact.title: Bronze Head of Queen Idia
artefact.summary: The Bronze Head of Queen Idia is a commemorative bronze head from the medieval Kingdom of Benin in West Africa that probably represents Idia , mother of Oba Esigie , made during the early sixteenth century at the Benin court. Many Benin works of art entered the European art market after the Benin Expedition of 1897 – Four cast bronze heads of the queen are known and are currently in the collections of the British Museum in London, [ 1 ] the World Museum in Liverpool, [ 2 ] the Nigerian National Museum in Lagos, [ 3 ] and the Ethnological Museum of Berlin . [ 4 ]
artefact.categories: Ethnographic objects in the British Museum; African sculptures in the British Museum; Bronze sculptures in London; Nigeria–United Kingdom relations; Germany–Nigeria relations; Benin Court Art; 16th-century sculptures

museum-experience_4.title: Musicians plate

artefact.title: Musicians plate
artefact.summary: Musicians plate ( Persian : بشقاب نوازندگان ) is a partially gilded silver plate or dish found in Tabaristan . [ 1 ] It was probably made in the 7th or 8th century in modern Iran , then under the Sasanian Empire , and is now in the British Museum . It shows a ruler reclining luxuriously, as he picnics while his musicians play. [ 2 ] The plain background is gilded, while the figures and objects in low relief are left in silver. The musician on the far right is playing a stringed instrument which could possibly be an oud or barbat .
artefact.categories: Archaeological discoveries in Iran; Middle Eastern objects in the British Museum; Mazandaran province; Gold objects; Silver objects; Persian art; Asian archaeology stubs; Iranian history stubs
artefact.infobox_raw: {"material": "Silver , Gold", "size": "Diameter: 19.7 centimetres Height: 4 centimetres", "created": "7th century", "period/culture": "Sasanid", "discovered": "Tabaristan", "present location": "British Museum , London", "identification": "1963,1210.3"}

museum-experience_5.title: Statue of Ashurnasirpal II

artefact.title: Statue of Ashurnasirpal II
artefact.summary: The Statue of Ashurnasirpal II is a rare example of Assyrian sculpture in the round that was found in the mid nineteenth century at the ancient site of Kalhu (now known as Nimrud ) by the famous archaeologist Austen Henry Layard . Dating from 883–859 BC, the statue has long been admired for its flawless condition and the high quality of its craftsmanship. It has been part of the British Museum 's collection since 1851. [ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]
artefact.categories: Middle Eastern sculptures in the British Museum; Assyrian art and architecture; Sculpture of the ancient Near East; Nimrud; Cultural depictions of Ashurbanipal

museum-experience_6.title: Guisborough Helmet

artefact.title: Guisborough Helmet
artefact.summary: The Guisborough Helmet is a Roman cavalry bronze helmet found in 1864 near Guisborough in the North Riding of Yorkshire , England. It was originally fitted with a pair of protective cheek-pieces, which have not survived; the holes by which they were attached can be seen in front of the helmet's ear guards. It is lavishly decorated with incised, punched and embossed figures, indicating that it was probably used for displays or cavalry tournaments, though it may well have been intended to be worn in battle as well. The helmet was found in what appears to have been a carefully arranged deposition in a bed of gravel, distant from any known Roman sites. After it was recovered during road works it was donated to the British Museum in London , where it was restored and is currently on display. [ 1 ]
artefact.discovery_site: By workers employed by the Cleveland Railway Company in 1864
artefact.categories: 3rd-century artifacts; 1864 archaeological discoveries; Ancient Roman helmets; Romano-British objects in the British Museum; Guisborough; Individual helmets; Roman armour from Britain; Archaeological discoveries in the United Kingdom
artefact.infobox_raw: {"material": "Bronze (copper alloy)", "size": "240 millimetres (9.4 in) high", "created": "3rd century AD", "period/culture": "Roman Empire", "discovered": "By workers employed by the Cleveland Railway Company in 1864", "place": "Guisborough , North Yorkshire", "present location": "Room 49, British Museum , London", "identification": "P&EE 1878 9-10 1", "registration": "1878,0910.1"}

museum-experience_7.title: Bell Shrine of Conall Cael

artefact.title: Bell Shrine of Conall Cael
artefact.summary: The Bell Shrine of Conall Cael (or Bell Shrine of Bernán Chonaill ) [ 3 ] is an early 12th century bell shrine found on Inishkeel island in County Donegal , Ireland. It was built to contain a 6th century bell associated with Conall Cael, a relatively obscure early Irish saint associated with Coolaun church, near Cashel in County Tipperary , and was probably created by a secular commissioner to give prestige to the locality. [ 4 ]
artefact.dimensions: Height: 21.9 cm (8.6 in) Width: 15.5 cm (6.1 in) Depth: 12.7 cm (5.0 in)

museum-experience_8.title: Copán Bench Panel

artefact.title: Copán Bench Panel
artefact.summary: The Copán Bench Panel or Copan Bench Panel is the name of a sculpted platform that was originally located in Temple 11 at the Maya site of Copán in Honduras . Discovered by the English archaeologist Alfred Maudslay in the late nineteenth century, it now forms part of the British Museum's extensive collection of ancient artefacts from Central America . [ 1 ] Dated to the late 8th Century AD, the panel is both a work of art and a significant historical record of royal authority.
artefact.dimensions: Length of left and right panels - c. 263 cm each

museum-experience_9.title: Empress pepper pot

artefact.title: Empress pepper pot
artefact.summary: The Hoxne hoard pepper pot , [ 1 ] commonly known as the Empress pepper pot , although it now seems not to represent an empress, is a silver piperatorium , partially gilded , dating from around 400 AD. It was found as part of the Hoxne Hoard in Hoxne, Suffolk , in November 1992, and is now in the British Museum , where it is normally on display. It is a hollow silver statuette of the top half of a woman's body, with a mechanism to allow ground pepper or spices to be loaded into its base and then shaken out. The mechanism does not grind the pepper and has a rotatable disc with three positions. One position allows filling, another has fine holes to allow ground pepper out and another allows the pot to be closed. [ 1 ] The pot was chosen as part of the 2010 BBC Radio 4 series, A History of the World in 100 Objects , as item 40. [ 2 ]
